topics.title: WordPress.org vs WordPress.com — What's the Difference?
topics.excerpt:
There are two separate WordPress platforms and they work very differently.

**WordPress.org (self-hosted)**
Free software you install on your own web hosting. You have full control — any plugin, any theme, any customization. Requires a hosting account ($5–20/month) and a domain name. You manage updates and backups. This is what most professional sites use, and what Dewey is designed to work with.

**WordPress.com (hosted service)**
A fully managed service run by Automattic. Has a free plan (with WordPress.com branding and a yoursite.wordpress.com address). Paid plans unlock custom domains and more features. Only Business plan and above allow third-party plugins. Easier to start, but far less control.

**How to tell which one you have:**
If you signed up through a web host (Bluehost, SiteGround, Kinsta, WP Engine, etc.) and access your admin at yoursite.com/wp-admin — you're on self-hosted WordPress.org.

If you signed up at WordPress.com directly and your address is yoursite.wordpress.com — you're on WordPress.com's hosted platform.

**Key difference:** WordPress.org gives you the full plugin directory and complete freedom. WordPress.com restricts plugins on lower plans.

topics.title: Posts vs Pages — When to Use Each
topics.excerpt:
**Posts** are time-stamped content entries — blog posts, news articles, updates. They appear in reverse chronological order, belong to categories and tags, show up in your RSS feed, and are designed for content that flows over time.

**Pages** are static, timeless content — your About, Contact, Services, or Home pages. They have no date, no categories or tags, can be organized in a parent/child hierarchy (e.g. About > Team), and don't appear in your RSS feed.

**Quick rule:** If it's content you'd read like a blog post, use a Post. If it's a permanent part of your site structure, use a Page.

| | Posts | Pages |
|---|---|---|
| Date shown | Yes | No |
| Categories & tags | Yes | No |
| RSS feed | Yes | No |
| Parent/child structure | No | Yes |
| Good for | Articles, news, updates | About, Contact, Home |

**Common mistake:** Creating a "Contact" post instead of a Contact page. Pages are the right choice for anything that's part of your site's permanent navigation.

topics.title: Post Statuses — Draft, Pending, Scheduled, Private, and More
topics.excerpt:
Every post and page has a status that controls who can see it and when.

**Draft** — Work in progress. Saved but invisible to visitors. The default state for a new post.

**Pending Review** — Submitted by an Author or Contributor and waiting for an Editor or Administrator to approve and publish. Appears in All Posts under the Pending tab.

**Published** — Live and visible to everyone on your site.

**Scheduled** — Set to go live at a future date and time. WordPress publishes it automatically at the scheduled moment. Set this by clicking the date in the 'Publish' panel of the editor before hitting Publish.

**Private** — Visible only to logged-in Editors and Administrators. Won't appear in archives, menus, or search results for regular visitors.

**Password Protected** — Publicly listed but requires a password to read. Set via Visibility in the post editor sidebar.

**Trashed** — Moved to trash. Not visible anywhere. Stays for 30 days before permanent deletion. Can be restored at any time from Posts > All Posts > Trash.

To change a status: open the post editor and look at 'Status & Visibility' in the right sidebar. Or use Quick Edit on the All Posts screen to change status without opening the full editor.

topics.title: Categories and Tags — How to Organize Your Posts
topics.excerpt:
Categories and tags are both ways to organize posts, but they work differently.

**Categories**
Hierarchical — you can have a parent category (News) with child categories (Local News, World News). Every post must have at least one category. If you don't assign one, WordPress uses 'Uncategorized'. Categories are broad topics. Use them for your main content buckets.

Manage them at Posts > Categories. Each gets a name, slug (used in the URL), optional parent, and optional description.

**Tags**
Flat — no hierarchy. Optional, and posts can have as many as needed. Tags are specific descriptors or keywords. Think of categories as the table of contents and tags as the index.

Both categories and tags create their own archive pages (e.g. yoursite.com/category/news/ or yoursite.com/tag/wordpress/).

**When to use each:**
- Categories: the main topic buckets every post belongs to (3–8 categories is typical)
- Tags: specific details worth linking across posts ('Gutenberg', 'Yoast', 'Speed')

**Tip:** Rename 'Uncategorized' in Settings > Writing to something that fits your site, or create a real default category. Posts that don't get categorized will fall into it.

topics.title: The Block Editor (Gutenberg) — How It Works
topics.excerpt:
The block editor (called Gutenberg) is the WordPress content editor. Everything in it is a 'block' — paragraphs, headings, images, buttons, columns — each independently editable.

**Adding blocks**
- Click the **+** button (top left or inline between blocks)
- Type **/** anywhere in the editor to search blocks by name (e.g. /image, /heading, /columns)
- Drag blocks from the inserter into position

**Editing blocks**
Click any block to select it. A toolbar appears above it with formatting options. Block-specific settings appear in the right sidebar under the 'Block' tab.

**Moving blocks**
Drag the six-dot handle on the left of a block, or use the up/down arrows in the block toolbar. The '...' menu on any block has 'Move to' for larger jumps.

**Key shortcuts**
- `Ctrl/Cmd + Z` — undo
- `Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + Z` — redo
- `Ctrl/Cmd + K` — add a link
- `/` — quick block search (must be in an empty paragraph)

**Useful views**
- **List View** (the three-line icon, top left) shows all blocks in a document tree — great for navigating complex layouts
- **Settings toggle** (the gear icon, top right) shows/hides the right sidebar
- **Fullscreen mode** — hides the admin bar for distraction-free writing (toggle in View menu)

**Post vs Block settings:** The right sidebar has two tabs. 'Post' settings (featured image, categories, status) and 'Block' settings (styling for the selected block). Click away from all blocks to see Post settings.

topics.title: Essential Blocks — Paragraph, Image, Heading, Columns, and More
topics.excerpt:
The most commonly used blocks in the WordPress editor:

**Text**
- **Paragraph** — basic text. Change font size and color in the right sidebar.
- **Heading** — H1 through H6. Set the level in the toolbar. Use H2 for main sections, H3 for sub-sections.
- **List** — ordered (numbered) or unordered (bullets). Toggle in toolbar. Supports nesting.
- **Quote / Pullquote** — styled blockquotes. Quote is inline; Pullquote is a large visual feature.
- **Table** — rows and columns. Add header/footer rows in the sidebar.

**Media**
- **Image** — upload, select from Media Library, or paste a URL. Set alt text in the sidebar (important for SEO and accessibility).
- **Gallery** — multiple images in a grid. Set column count and crop settings.
- **Cover** — image or video with text overlaid. Good for hero sections.
- **Media & Text** — image on one side, text on the other.
- **Video** — upload a video or embed from a URL.

**Layout**
- **Columns** — side-by-side content areas. Set column count and widths.
- **Group** — wrap blocks together so you can set a background color, padding, or move them as one unit.
- **Buttons** — clickable call-to-action buttons. Set link, style (filled or outline), colors.
- **Separator** — horizontal dividing line.
- **Spacer** — adds vertical white space. Set height in sidebar.

**Embeds**
Paste a YouTube, Vimeo, Twitter, or other supported URL on its own line and WordPress auto-converts it to an embed block.

topics.title: Featured Images — Setting and Removing the Post Thumbnail
topics.excerpt:
A featured image is the primary image associated with a post or page. Themes use it in post listings, social sharing previews, and often at the top of the post itself.

**Setting a featured image:**
1. Open the post editor
2. In the right sidebar, click the 'Post' tab (click away from all blocks if you don't see it)
3. Scroll down to 'Featured Image' and click 'Set featured image'
4. Upload a new image or choose one from the Media Library
5. Click 'Set featured image' in the media modal

**Removing a featured image:**
In the same Featured Image panel, click 'Remove featured image' below the thumbnail preview.

**Image size tips:**
- Use at least 1200×630 pixels for good display across themes and social sharing
- Landscape (wider than tall) generally works best for most themes
- The theme controls how the image is displayed and cropped — check your theme's documentation for recommended sizes

**Not showing up?** Some themes don't display featured images on every post type, or only show them on archive pages. If the image appears set in the editor but not on the front end, it's likely a theme setting — check Appearance > Customize or the theme documentation.

**SEO note:** Most SEO plugins (Yoast, Rank Math) use the featured image as the Open Graph image for social sharing previews.

topics.title: Media Library — Managing Images, Files, and Alt Text
topics.excerpt:
The Media Library (Media > Library) is where all uploaded images, documents, and files are stored.

**Uploading files**
Drag and drop files onto the Media Library page, or click 'Add New'. Files can also be uploaded directly from the block editor when you add an image or file block.

**Alt text — the most important field**
Alt text describes an image to people using screen readers and to search engines. Every image should have it. To set it:
1. Click the image in the Media Library
2. Find the 'Alt Text' field on the right and type a clear description
3. Click 'Update' to save

Good alt text: 'Woman typing on a laptop at a coffee shop'
Bad alt text: 'image001', 'photo', or leaving it blank

**Image sizes**
WordPress automatically creates multiple sizes when you upload an image:
- **Thumbnail** — 150×150 (cropped square)
- **Medium** — up to 300×300
- **Medium Large** — up to 768px wide
- **Large** — up to 1024×1024
- **Full** — original dimensions

Change default sizes at Settings > Media.

**Editing images**
Click an image and hit 'Edit Image' to crop, rotate, flip, or scale it without leaving WordPress.

**Deleting files**
Click the image, then 'Delete Permanently'. This removes the file from the server — make sure it's not in use anywhere on the site first. WordPress will warn you if it finds the image embedded in posts.

topics.title: User Roles — What Each Role Can Do
topics.excerpt:
WordPress has five built-in user roles, each with different levels of access:

**Administrator**
Full access to everything — plugins, themes, settings, all content, and all users. Reserve this role for site owners and trusted developers only.

**Editor**
Can create, edit, publish, and delete any content (including other users' posts). Can manage categories and moderate comments. Cannot install plugins or change themes.

**Author**
Can write, edit, publish, and delete their own posts only. Can upload media. Cannot edit anyone else's posts.

**Contributor**
Can write and edit their own posts, but cannot publish them — posts go to Pending Review for an Editor or Administrator to approve. Cannot upload media.

**Subscriber**
Can log in and manage their own profile. Can read password-protected content. No ability to create content.

**Summary table:**
| Role | Publish own posts | Edit others' posts | Manage settings |
|---|---|---|---|
| Administrator | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Editor | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Author | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Contributor | ✗ (pending) | ✗ | ✗ |
| Subscriber | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |

**Changing a user's role:** Go to Users > All Users, hover over a username, and click 'Edit'. Change the role dropdown and click 'Update User'. Or select multiple users and use Bulk Actions > Change role.

**Plugins like MemberPress or Restrict Content Pro** can add additional custom roles beyond these defaults.

topics.title: Themes — Installing, Switching, and Customizing Your Site's Look
topics.excerpt:
Your theme controls the visual design of your entire site — layout, colors, fonts, header, and footer.

**Installing a theme**
1. Go to Appearance > Themes > Add New
2. Browse the WordPress.org directory, or upload a .zip file for premium themes
3. Click 'Install' then 'Activate'

**Two types of themes**

*Classic themes* use the Customizer (Appearance > Customize) for visual changes. The Customizer shows a live preview. Common panels: Site Identity (logo, site name), Colors, Header, Menus, Widgets, Homepage Settings.

*Block themes* use the Site Editor (Appearance > Editor) instead. Block themes let you edit every part of the site — headers, footers, templates — using the same block editor you use for posts. Most newer themes are block themes.

**Switching themes safely**
- Your content (posts, pages, media) is never lost when you switch themes
- Theme-specific settings (custom menus, widget placements) may need to be reconfigured
- Test on a staging site before switching on a live site

**Child themes**
If you want to customize a theme with code, use a child theme — it inherits the parent theme and keeps your changes when the parent updates. Without a child theme, theme updates overwrite your edits.

**Style variations** (block themes only): Many block themes include pre-built color and font combinations you can switch between at Appearance > Editor > Styles.

topics.title: Full Site Editing (FSE) — Site Editor, Templates, and Template Parts
topics.excerpt:
Full Site Editing (FSE) lets you edit every part of your site — headers, footers, templates — using the block editor. It's available when you're using a block theme.

**Access it at Appearance > Editor** (not Appearance > Customize — that's for classic themes).

**Navigation**
Edit your site's navigation menus with the Navigation block. Add links, reorder them, and configure the mobile menu behavior.

**Styles (Global Styles)**
Set site-wide typography (fonts and sizes for headings and body text), color palette, spacing, and per-block default styles. Click the half-circle styles icon in the top right of the Site Editor. Some block themes offer pre-built 'style variations' — different color and font combinations to switch between.

**Templates**
Templates control the layout used for different content types. Common templates: Front Page, Single Post, Single Page, Archive, Search Results, 404. Edit a template to change the layout for all content using it. Go to Editor > Templates to see and edit them.

**Template Parts**
Reusable sections shared across templates — typically Header and Footer. Edit the header template part once and it updates everywhere it's used. Go to Editor > Patterns > Template Parts.

**Synced Patterns**
Reusable block groups that stay in sync — edit one instance and all instances update. Useful for repeated content like CTAs or announcement bars.

**Resetting changes:** Any template or style changes can be reset to the theme's defaults. Look for 'Reset to default' in the template or styles options.

topics.title: Menus — Creating and Managing Navigation
topics.excerpt:
**Classic themes use Appearance > Menus.**

**Creating a menu:**
1. Go to Appearance > Menus
2. Give the menu a name and click 'Create Menu'
3. Add items from the left panels: Pages, Posts, Custom Links, Categories, Tags
4. Check items and click 'Add to Menu'
5. Drag items to reorder. Nest an item slightly to the right to make it a sub-menu (dropdown)
6. Under 'Menu Settings', assign it to a Menu Location (Primary Menu, Footer, etc.)
7. Click 'Save Menu'

**Menu item settings:**
Expand any menu item with the arrow to set: Navigation Label (what visitors see), Title Attribute (tooltip on hover), Open in New Tab, CSS Classes, Description.

**Custom links:**
Use Custom Links to link to external URLs, specific sections of your site, or any URL that isn't a post or page.

**Menu locations** are defined by your theme. A theme might have 'Primary Navigation', 'Footer Menu', and 'Social Links'. If a location isn't assigned a menu, the theme may show nothing or a default fallback.

**Block themes use the Navigation block.**
For block themes, manage navigation in Appearance > Editor > Navigation. Add and reorder links directly in the block editor interface instead of the classic Menus screen.

**Can't find Menus?** If Appearance > Menus is missing, you're using a block theme — look for navigation in Appearance > Editor.

topics.title: Homepage Settings — Static Page vs Latest Posts
topics.excerpt:
By default, WordPress shows your latest posts on the homepage. Most sites want a custom homepage instead.

**Setting a static homepage:**
1. Create two pages: one for your homepage content (e.g. 'Home') and one for your blog (e.g. 'Blog')
2. Go to Settings > Reading
3. Under 'Your homepage displays', select 'A static page'
4. Set 'Homepage' to your Home page
5. Set 'Posts page' to your Blog page
6. Click 'Save Changes'

Your homepage will now show the content of your Home page, and your Blog page will show posts in reverse chronological order.

**If you only want a homepage with no blog:**
Create a Home page, set it as the Homepage in Settings > Reading, and leave 'Posts page' blank. You won't have a blog archive, but that's fine if you don't need one.

**Block themes:** You can also set this in Appearance > Editor by editing the Front Page and Home templates. But Settings > Reading is the most reliable place to control this.

**Common issue:** You created a page called 'Home' but the homepage still shows posts. This happens because you didn't set it in Settings > Reading — creating the page isn't enough.

**Blog pages show at most:** Also in Settings > Reading, you can change how many posts appear per page in archives (default is 10).

topics.title: Permalinks — URL Structure for Posts and Pages
topics.excerpt:
Permalinks are the URL structure WordPress uses for posts and pages. Set them at Settings > Permalinks.

**Recommended structure: Post name**
This gives you clean URLs like `yoursite.com/my-post-title/` — best for SEO and readability.

**All options:**
| Structure | Example |
|---|---|
| Plain | yoursite.com/?p=123 |
| Day and name | yoursite.com/2024/01/15/post-title/ |
| Month and name | yoursite.com/2024/01/post-title/ |
| Numeric | yoursite.com/archives/123 |
| **Post name** *(recommended)* | yoursite.com/post-title/ |
| Custom | Build with date, category, post name tags |

**Changing the permalink of a single post:**
In the post editor, click the slug (shown below the title or in the URL field in the sidebar) and edit it. This only changes that one post's URL.

**If changing structure breaks URLs (404 errors):**
Go to Settings > Permalinks and click 'Save Changes' — this flushes WordPress's URL routing rules even if you didn't change the setting. This fixes most permalink-related 404s.

**Don't change structure on a live site without redirects.** If you change from one structure to another on an established site, old links and bookmarks will break. Use a redirect plugin (like Redirection) to forward old URLs to new ones.

**Category and Tag bases:**
At the bottom of Settings > Permalinks you can change the base prefix for category and tag archives (default: /category/ and /tag/).

topics.title: Scheduling Posts — Publishing at a Future Date and Time
topics.excerpt:
WordPress can automatically publish posts at a future date and time.

**How to schedule a post:**
1. Write your post as normal
2. In the right sidebar, find 'Status & Visibility' (click away from all blocks if you don't see it)
3. Click the date/time shown next to 'Publish' (it defaults to the current time)
4. Set your desired future date and time
5. Click 'Schedule' (the Publish button changes to Schedule once a future date is set)

**The timezone matters.** WordPress uses the timezone set in Settings > General. Make sure it's set correctly before scheduling.

**Viewing scheduled posts:**
Go to Posts > All Posts and click the 'Scheduled' filter to see all upcoming posts and their publish times.

**Missed schedule?** If WordPress misses a scheduled post (shows 'Missed schedule' status), it usually means the site had no visitor traffic at the scheduled time — WordPress uses visitor requests to trigger its scheduler (WP-Cron). A plugin like WP Scheduled Posts or asking your host about a real server cron job can fix this reliably.

**Changing a scheduled post:**
Open the post, click the date in the sidebar, change it, and click 'Update Schedule'. To publish it immediately instead, change the date to now and click 'Publish Now'.

topics.title: Comments — Moderation, Spam, and Discussion Settings
topics.excerpt:
**Managing comments is done at Comments > All Comments.**

The tabs show: All, Pending (awaiting approval), Approved, Spam, and Trash. Pending comments are the ones that need your attention.

**Approving or rejecting a comment:**
Hover over a comment to see actions: Approve, Reply, Quick Edit, Edit, Spam, Trash. Approved comments appear on the post immediately. Marking as Spam teaches the spam filter.

**Bulk actions:**
Select multiple comments and use Bulk Actions to approve, mark as spam, or trash them at once.

**Comment settings (Settings > Discussion):**
- *Allow comments* — turn off globally for new posts (existing posts keep their setting)
- *Require name and email* — reduces anonymous spam
- *Registered users only* — only logged-in users can comment
- *Hold for moderation* — all comments go to pending (safest option)
- *Email me when* — get notifications for new comments and comments in moderation
- *Close comments after X days* — automatically lock old posts

**Disabling comments on a specific post:**
In the post editor, go to the 'Discussion' panel (may need to enable it via the three-dot menu > Preferences > Panels). Uncheck 'Allow comments'.

**Akismet** is the standard spam-filtering plugin. Install it from Plugins > Add New and connect it with a free API key from akismet.com. It automatically catches most spam.

**Turning off comments site-wide:**
Go to Settings > Discussion and uncheck 'Allow people to submit comments on new posts'. For existing posts, use Bulk Edit in Posts > All Posts to disable comments in bulk.

topics.title: Plugins — Installing, Activating, and Troubleshooting
topics.excerpt:
Plugins add functionality to WordPress. There are 60,000+ free plugins at wordpress.org, plus thousands of premium ones sold directly or on marketplaces like CodeCanyon.

**Installing a free plugin:**
1. Go to Plugins > Add New
2. Search for the plugin name
3. Click 'Install Now' → then 'Activate'

**Installing a premium plugin (from a .zip file):**
1. Download the .zip file from the developer or marketplace
2. Go to Plugins > Add New > 'Upload Plugin'
3. Choose the .zip file and click 'Install Now'
4. Activate after installation

**Deactivating vs Deleting**
Deactivating a plugin turns it off but keeps its files and settings. Deleting removes the files (and sometimes the data — check the plugin's documentation). Always deactivate before deleting.

**Keeping plugins updated:**
Go to Dashboard > Updates or Plugins > Installed Plugins and look for the 'Update Available' notice. Update plugins one at a time on important sites — bulk updates are fine on simpler sites. Enable auto-updates per-plugin in Installed Plugins (toggle in the Auto-updates column).

**Diagnosing a plugin conflict:**
If something breaks after installing a plugin, deactivate the new plugin first. If problems started without installing anything, deactivate all plugins, then reactivate them one by one until the problem returns — that's your conflict.

**Quality signals when choosing a plugin:**
- Active installations (more = more tested)
- Star rating and number of reviews
- 'Last updated' date (more than 2 years = risky)
- 'Tested up to' (should match your WordPress version)

topics.title: Updates and Backups — Keeping Your Site Safe
topics.excerpt:
**Keeping WordPress updated is the single most important security practice.**

**Update types:**
- *Minor updates* (e.g. 6.4.1 → 6.4.2) — security and bug fixes. WordPress installs these automatically by default. Safe to let happen.
- *Major updates* (e.g. 6.4 → 6.5) — new features. Update manually after testing.

**Where to update:** Dashboard > Updates shows everything pending: WordPress core, plugins, and themes.

**Best practice for updates:**
1. Back up first (always)
2. Update plugins and themes first, then WordPress core
3. Test the site after updating — check the homepage, a post, and your contact form

**Backups — the most neglected WordPress task.**
WordPress does NOT back up your site automatically. You need a plugin.

**Popular backup plugins:**
- **UpdraftPlus** — most popular; free tier backs up on demand or schedule; can save to Dropbox, Google Drive, S3
- **BackWPup** — free, flexible, good for automated backups
- **All-in-One WP Migration** — good for moving sites as well as backup

**A complete backup needs two things:**
1. The **database** — all your posts, pages, settings, and users
2. The **files** — themes, plugins, and your uploaded media (/wp-content/uploads/)

**Store backups off-site.** Saving backups only on your server means if the server fails, you lose the backups too. Store them in Dropbox, Google Drive, S3, or download to your computer.

**Before any major change** — new theme, major plugin install, site migration — run a manual backup first.

topics.title: SEO — Yoast, Rank Math, and Basic Optimization
topics.excerpt:
SEO plugins help search engines understand and rank your content. The top choices are Yoast SEO and Rank Math — both are excellent, and you only need one.

**Yoast SEO** (free + premium)
- Adds a panel below every post editor with SEO title, meta description, and focus keyword analysis
- Scores readability and SEO with a traffic-light system (red/orange/green)
- Automatically generates an XML sitemap at yoursite.com/sitemap_index.xml
- Handles Open Graph tags for social sharing previews

**Rank Math** (free + premium)
- Similar to Yoast but more features in the free tier
- Allows multiple focus keywords per post (free)
- Built-in schema markup, 404 monitor, and redirections in free version

**Basic SEO for every post:**
- **SEO title** — what appears in Google results; include your primary keyword near the front
- **Meta description** — the snippet under the title in search results; write 150–160 characters that sell the click
- **Slug** — keep it short and descriptive (e.g. /wordpress-seo-guide not /a-complete-guide-to-seo-for-wordpress-sites)
- **Alt text** on all images
- **Heading hierarchy** — one H1 (usually the post title), H2 for main sections, H3 for sub-points

**Sitemaps**
Once installed, SEO plugins generate sitemaps automatically. Submit the sitemap URL to Google Search Console (search.google.com/search-console) so Google can find all your content.

**Settings > Reading warning:** Make sure 'Discourage search engines from indexing this site' is unchecked on live sites — this option adds a noindex tag that hides your entire site from Google.

topics.title: WordPress Security — Essential Steps for Site Owners
topics.excerpt:
**The most impactful security steps, in order of importance:**

**1. Keep everything updated**
Outdated plugins and themes are the #1 cause of WordPress hacks. Update regularly. Dashboard > Updates.

**2. Use strong, unique passwords**
WordPress's built-in strong password generator is good. Never reuse passwords across sites. Use a password manager.

**3. Don't use 'admin' as a username**
If your administrator username is 'admin', create a new admin account with a different name, transfer your content, and delete the 'admin' account. Bots specifically target the 'admin' username.

**4. Enable HTTPS (SSL)**
If your site doesn't start with https://, contact your host — most offer free SSL via Let's Encrypt. The 'Really Simple SSL' plugin can handle the WordPress configuration side.

**5. Install a security plugin**
- **Wordfence** — free; includes a firewall, malware scanner, and login security (blocks brute-force attempts)
- **Sucuri Security** — good malware scanning and site monitoring
- **Limit Login Attempts Reloaded** — lightweight; just blocks repeated failed logins

**6. Use two-factor authentication (2FA)**
Wordfence, iThemes Security, and other plugins add 2FA to the WordPress login screen.

**7. Take regular backups**
If you get hacked, a clean backup is how you recover. See the Updates and Backups topic.

**Signs your site may be compromised:** visitors being redirected elsewhere, Google showing a 'Site may be hacked' warning, new admin users you didn't create, unusual files in wp-content.

topics.title: Common WordPress Problems and How to Fix Them
topics.excerpt:
**404 errors on posts/pages**
Go to Settings > Permalinks and click 'Save Changes' — this flushes WordPress's URL rules without changing anything. Fixes most post 404s.

**Can't log in / lost password**
Use the 'Lost your password?' link on the wp-login.php screen. WordPress emails a reset link to your registered email address. If you can't receive email, ask your host to reset it via the database.

**White screen of death (blank white page)**
Usually a PHP error. Check if it's admin-only or site-wide. The most common cause is a plugin or theme with a fatal error. Try adding `?nowprocket` to the URL or access via a different browser. Contact your host to check PHP error logs. If you can access wp-admin, deactivate plugins.

**Images not displaying**
Check that images were actually uploaded correctly. Permissions issue on /wp-content/uploads/ can prevent images from serving — contact your host. A broken image in the media library can sometimes be fixed by re-uploading.

**Site stuck in maintenance mode**
WordPress creates a `.maintenance` file during updates. If an update failed partway, it may stay. Connect via FTP or your host's file manager and delete the `.maintenance` file from the root WordPress directory.

**Too many redirects**
Often caused by a mismatch between 'WordPress Address' and 'Site Address' in Settings > General, or an SSL plugin conflict. Check those URLs match and are both http or both https.

**Plugin conflict**
If something breaks unexpectedly, deactivate all plugins and reactivate one by one to find the culprit.

**Comments not showing up after approval**
Check Settings > Discussion for moderation settings, and check the spam folder in Comments > All Comments > Spam.

topics.title: WooCommerce — WordPress E-Commerce Overview
topics.excerpt:
WooCommerce is the most popular WordPress e-commerce plugin, free to install and powering millions of online stores.

**What it adds to WordPress:**
- Products (simple, variable, grouped, external/affiliate)
- Shopping cart and checkout
- Order management
- Payment gateways (Stripe and PayPal built-in; many more available)
- Shipping zones and rates
- Tax settings
- Coupon/discount management
- Customer accounts

**Getting started:**
1. Install WooCommerce from Plugins > Add New
2. Follow the Setup Wizard — it creates Shop, Cart, Checkout, and My Account pages automatically
3. Add your first product at Products > Add New
4. Connect a payment gateway (WooCommerce > Settings > Payments)

**Product types:**
- *Simple* — single product, no variations (e.g. a book)
- *Variable* — product with options like size and color (each combination can have a different price/stock)
- *Grouped* — a collection of related products
- *External/Affiliate* — links out to another site for purchase
- *Downloadable* — digital files delivered after payment

**Managing orders:** WooCommerce > Orders. Order statuses: Pending, Processing, On Hold, Completed, Cancelled, Refunded, Failed.

**Common paid extensions:** WooCommerce Subscriptions (recurring payments), WooCommerce Bookings (appointments), WooCommerce Product Add-Ons (custom fields).

**Performance note:** WooCommerce adds significant complexity to a WordPress site. Use a caching plugin that's WooCommerce-aware (WP Rocket or LiteSpeed Cache) and skip cart/checkout pages from caching.

topics.title: Block Patterns and Synced Patterns — Saving and Reusing Block Layouts
topics.excerpt:
Patterns let you save layouts and content for reuse across your site.

**Block Patterns**
Pre-designed layouts made of multiple blocks. Find them in the block inserter under the 'Patterns' tab. Your active theme usually includes patterns for heroes, CTAs, featured content sections, and more. Insert a pattern as a starting point and customize it — it doesn't stay connected to the original.

**Synced Patterns (formerly Reusable Blocks)**
Save any block or group of blocks as a synced pattern and use it in multiple places. When you edit the synced pattern in one place, it updates everywhere. Perfect for: promotional banners, contact CTAs, disclaimer text that needs to be consistent across posts.

**Creating a synced pattern:**
1. Select the block(s) you want to save
2. Click the three-dot '...' menu on the block toolbar
3. Choose 'Create pattern'
4. Give it a name; make sure 'Synced' is checked
5. Click 'Create'

The pattern now appears in the Patterns tab of the inserter and in Appearance > Editor > Patterns.

**Using a pattern without syncing:**
If you want a pattern as a starting point but don't want changes to propagate, create it with 'Synced' unchecked, or after inserting a synced pattern click '...' > 'Detach' to unlink it from the original.

**Managing patterns:**
All saved patterns are managed at Appearance > Editor > Patterns (block themes) or via the Patterns section in the block inserter.

topics.title: Import and Export — Moving Content Between WordPress Sites
topics.excerpt:
WordPress has built-in tools for moving content between sites.

**Exporting content (Tools > Export)**
Downloads a WXR (.xml) file containing your selected content.
- *All Content* — posts, pages, comments, categories, tags, custom fields, and users
- *Posts only* — filter by category, author, date range, or status
- *Pages only*
- *Media* — just media library attachments

Important: the export file contains **content only** — not your theme, plugins, or media files. Images referenced in posts won't be in the XML; they're downloaded from your old site during import.

**Importing content (Tools > Import)**
Choose 'WordPress' from the list. If the WordPress Importer plugin isn't installed, WordPress will prompt you to install it.

During import:
- You can assign imported content to an existing user or create a new one
- Check 'Download and import file attachments' to have WordPress fetch referenced media from the old site

**Limitations of the built-in exporter:**
Doesn't export: theme settings, plugin data, customizer settings, widgets, menus, or database tables added by plugins.

**For a full site migration (everything including theme/plugin settings):**
Use a migration plugin:
- **All-in-One WP Migration** — simple, works well for most sites
- **Duplicator** — more control, good for large sites
- **WP Migrate** — developer-focused, handles database and files separately

**After importing:** reassign any 'auto-draft' or unassigned featured images, check menus and widgets, and flush permalinks (Settings > Permalinks > Save).

topics.title: The WordPress Dashboard and Admin Bar
topics.excerpt:
**The Dashboard** is the home screen of wp-admin, accessed at yoursite.com/wp-admin.

**Dashboard widgets:**
- *At a Glance* — quick count of posts, pages, comments, and your current WordPress/theme version
- *Activity* — recently published posts and pending comments
- *Quick Draft* — write a draft without going to the full editor
- *WordPress Events and News* — upcoming WordCamps and WordPress announcements

The welcome panel shown to new sites can be dismissed with the 'Dismiss' link.

**The Admin Bar** appears at the top of wp-admin and on the front end of your site when you're logged in.

Key items in the admin bar:
- **+ New** (left side) — quickly create a new Post, Page, Media upload, or User without navigating menus
- **Site name** — links back to the Dashboard
- **Comments icon** — badge shows pending comments count; click to go to Comments
- **Updates icon** — badge shows available updates count; click to go to Dashboard > Updates
- **Your name** (right side) — click to edit your profile or log out
- **Customize** (front end only) — opens the Customizer for the current page

**Hiding the admin bar:**
The admin bar shows on the site's front end when logged in. To hide it, go to Users > Profile and uncheck 'Show Toolbar when viewing site'.

**Dashboard color schemes:**
In Users > Profile, you can choose from 8 color themes for the wp-admin interface (Default, Light, Modern, Blue, Coffee, Ectoplasm, Midnight, Ocean, Sunrise).
